positive_description: Right-lateralized lower face and jaw deformation. The mirror of psi19 — a barely perceptible right-side jaw positioning or lower face firming. At the weights tested (+3.0), the effect is at the threshold of visual discrimination in static renders.
negative_description: Right-lateralized lower face relaxation. A barely perceptible softening of the right jaw area. Visually near-indistinguishable from neutral at tested weights.
symmetry_notes: Asymmetric — the right-sided member of the psi19/psi20 conjugate pair, mirroring psi19 across the sagittal plane. The left/right 3/4 views show no clearly discernible lateralization at these weights, consistent with the effect being below reliable visual threshold.
artifact_notes: No mesh artifacts at +/-3.0. Component is too subtle at these weights to produce artifacts. Likely safe well beyond the tested range.
primary_face_region: right jaw, right lower face, right chin area
useful_for: bilateral lower face expression (as conjugate pair with psi19)

interactions.psi19: CONJUGATE PAIR. psi19+psi20 at equal weights produces bilateral lower face/jaw expression.
interactions.psi17_18: Same conjugate architecture — psi19/20 is the fourth pair, following psi17/18 (mid-face), psi14/15 (brows), psi11/12 (mouth corners)
interactions.psi0: psi0 drives primary symmetric jaw drop; psi19/20 provides fine-grained asymmetric jaw control

prose: Psi20 is the right-handed mirror of psi19, completing the fourth conjugate pair in the systematic left/right decomposition of FLAME expression space. At the tested weights (-3.0 to +3.0), the deformation is at or below the threshold of reliable visual discrimination in static frontal renders — all five frontal views and both 3/4 views appear essentially identical to the neutral baseline. This is consistent with the steady decline in PCA variance: by component 20, each component captures an extremely small fraction of training-set expression variance. The structural role (right-sided jaw/lower-face counterpart to psi19) is inferred from the conjugate pair architecture established in earlier components.
